content.text: Report generation agents take raw evidence, data, or research findings and autonomously produce structured, formatted, cited reports. They implement a plan-write-review cycle: first generating a document outline based on the input material and report type, then writing each section in parallel with consistent style and citation standards, then running quality checks for internal consistency, appropriate length, and factual accuracy before delivering a polished output document.

attrs.alt: Report Generation Agent: input data → outline planner → section writer (parallel) → quality checker → citation injector + formatter → final report

content.text: Report generation in the context of AI agents refers to the automated production of structured documents — research summaries, business intelligence reports, competitive analyses, technical assessments, or regulatory filings — from structured or unstructured input data. A report generation agent does more than call a large language model with a 'write me a report' prompt: it maintains document structure across sections, enforces consistent tone and citation standards, manages section length targets, handles cross-references, and optionally renders the output to multiple formats (Markdown, HTML, PDF, DOCX).

The key challenge that distinguishes report generation from simple text generation is coherence at scale. A 20-section, 10,000-word report cannot be generated in a single LLM context window — the output becomes incoherent, sections repeat information or contradict each other, and the document loses structural integrity. Report generation agents solve this by planning the document structure first, writing sections independently, and then running a consistency-checking pass that detects contradictions, repairs cross-references, and enforces style guidelines.

Report generation agents integrate closely with upstream research pipelines (deep research agents, literature review agents, data analysis agents) that provide the evidence base. The report generator's job is to take a structured evidence store and produce a human-readable document that a non-expert can follow, with clear section hierarchy, appropriate citations, executive summaries, and visualisation specifications.

Knowledge workers spend a disproportionate amount of time on report writing relative to the analytical work that precedes it. A research analyst who spends 3 days gathering evidence may spend 2 more days writing a report that structures and presents those findings. Automating the report writing step allows analysts to focus on the higher-value tasks of questioning assumptions, designing studies, and communicating insights to stakeholders.

Standardisation is a secondary benefit. Manual reports written by different analysts reflect individual style choices, varying levels of detail, and inconsistent citation practices. An automated report generation system applies the same template, style guidelines, and citation standards to every report, making a corpus of 1,000 reports as consistent as a single author's work. This is particularly valuable for regulatory submissions, audit reports, and investment memos where consistency is a quality criterion.
Speed: generates a 15-section report from an evidence store in 2-5 minutes vs. 2+ days manually.
Consistency: applies identical style, terminology, citation format, and section structure to every report.
Scalability: runs 100 reports overnight from a batch of evidence stores without incremental effort.
Multi-format output: generates Markdown, HTML, PDF, and DOCX from the same source with format-specific renderers.
Citation accuracy: citations are injected from a structured evidence store, eliminating manual reference management errors.
Iterative refinement: agents can revise specific sections based on feedback without regenerating the entire document.

Report quality is bounded by input evidence quality. A report generation agent that receives thin, low-quality evidence will produce a thin, low-quality report — it cannot fabricate substantive content. This means the report generator is only as good as the upstream research pipeline feeding it evidence.
Hallucination in transition paragraphs: LLMs filling in logical connections between section points may fabricate bridging claims not present in the evidence.
Repetition across sections: without explicit deduplication constraints, the model may restate the same finding across multiple sections.
Shallow analysis: automated reports may list findings without providing the deeper interpretive analysis that expert analysts add.
Style mismatch: fine-tuning or detailed style prompts are needed to match specific organisation style guides.
Long documents require multiple LLM calls: context window limits force section-by-section generation, which can lose global coherence.

The pipeline begins with outline planning. The agent receives the evidence store (structured as a list of findings with source citations) and the report type specification (executive summary, technical report, regulatory submission). The planning LLM generates a hierarchical outline with section titles, brief descriptions of what each section should cover, and a target word count.

Each section is then written in parallel by separate LLM calls, each receiving only the relevant subset of the evidence store (filtered by section topic) to avoid context bloat. A section header and context from adjacent sections is included to maintain continuity. Parallel section writing is the primary throughput optimisation: a 15-section report written sequentially takes 3-4 minutes; parallel writing reduces this to 60-90 seconds.

The consistency checking pass reads the full assembled draft and identifies: (1) factual contradictions between sections; (2) undefined references (mentions of findings not introduced earlier); (3) sections that are significantly too long or too short relative to their target; and (4) missing cross-references that should link related sections. The agent then makes targeted edits to resolve each identified issue without regenerating entire sections.

The diagram above shows the report generation pipeline. Input Data (evidence store, data, findings) flows to the Outline Planner which produces a structured document skeleton. The Section Writer generates each section in parallel. The Quality Checker reviews the assembled draft for consistency and completeness. The Citation Injector links claims to source evidence. The Formatter renders the output to the target format. Feedback loops from the Quality Checker allow section regeneration when quality checks fail.
When to Use Report Generation Agents
Recurring reports: weekly/monthly business summaries, market reports, security threat digests.
Research synthesis: converting a literature review agent's evidence store into a publishable summary.
Competitive intelligence reports: structuring findings from a web research agent into a formatted briefing.
Data analytics reports: translating SQL query results and statistical outputs into narrative reports.
Compliance and audit reports: generating standardised reports from structured audit logs.
Technical documentation: producing API reference documents or architecture overviews from code + design specs.

import anthropic
import asyncio
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any

@dataclass
class ReportSection:
    title: str
    description: str
    target_words: int
    evidence_keys: list[str]
    content: str = ""

@dataclass
class ReportSpec:
    title: str
    audience: str
    report_type: str
    sections: list[ReportSection] = field(default_factory=list)

class ReportGenerationAgent:
    def __init__(self, evidence_store: dict[str, str]):
        self.evidence = evidence_store
        self.client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    def plan_outline(self, objective: str, report_type: str, audience: str) -> ReportSpec:
        """Generate a structured outline for the report."""
        evidence_summary = "
".join(
            f"- {k}: {v[:200]}" for k, v in list(self.evidence.items())[:20]
        )
        response = self.client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=1024,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"""
Plan a {report_type} report outline.

Title: {objective}
Audience: {audience}
Evidence available:
{evidence_summary}

Return JSON:
{{
  "title": "...",
  "sections": [
    {{"title": "...", "description": "...", "target_words": 300, "evidence_keys": ["key1"]}}
  ]
}}
"""}]
        )
        text = response.content[0].text
        plan = json.loads(text[text.find("{"):text.rfind("}")+1])
        sections = [ReportSection(**s) for s in plan.get("sections", [])]
        return ReportSpec(
            title=plan.get("title", objective),
            audience=audience,
            report_type=report_type,
            sections=sections
        )

    def write_section(self, spec: ReportSpec, section: ReportSection) -> str:
        """Write a single report section."""
        section_evidence = "
".join(
            f"[{k}] {self.evidence.get(k, '')[:500]}"
            for k in section.evidence_keys
            if k in self.evidence
        )
        response = self.client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=section.target_words * 2,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"""
Write the "{section.title}" section of a {spec.report_type} report for {spec.audience}.

Section description: {section.description}
Target length: ~{section.target_words} words

Evidence (cite by key):
{section_evidence or "General knowledge applicable."}

Requirements:
- Use inline citations like [key1], [key2]
- Stay within target word count
- Professional, clear prose
"""}]
        )
        return response.content[0].text

    async def write_sections_parallel(self, spec: ReportSpec) -> None:
        """Write all sections in parallel."""
        loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
        tasks = []
        for section in spec.sections:
            task = loop.run_in_executor(None, self.write_section, spec, section)
            tasks.append(task)
        results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
        for section, content in zip(spec.sections, results):
            section.content = content

    def quality_check(self, spec: ReportSpec) -> list[str]:
        """Check assembled report for consistency issues."""
        full_draft = "

".join([
            f"## {s.title}
{s.content}" for s in spec.sections
        ])
        response = self.client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=512,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"""
Review this report draft for quality issues.
Return a JSON list of issues: [{{"section": "...", "issue": "...", "severity": "high/medium/low"}}]

Title: {spec.title}
Draft (first 3000 chars):
{full_draft[:3000]}
"""}]
        )
        text = response.content[0].text
        start, end = text.find("["), text.rfind("]") + 1
        if start >= 0:
            return json.loads(text[start:end])
        return []

    def format_markdown(self, spec: ReportSpec) -> str:
        """Render the report as Markdown."""
        lines = [f"# {spec.title}
"]
        lines.append(f"**Report Type:** {spec.report_type}")
        lines.append(f"**Audience:** {spec.audience}
")
        lines.append("---
")
        for section in spec.sections:
            lines.append(f"## {section.title}
")
            lines.append(section.content)
            lines.append("
")
        return "
".join(lines)

    async def generate(self, objective: str, report_type: str = "executive summary", audience: str = "senior management") -> str:
        """Full report generation pipeline."""
        spec = self.plan_outline(objective, report_type, audience)
        print(f"Planned {len(spec.sections)} sections")

        await self.write_sections_parallel(spec)
        print("Sections written")

        issues = self.quality_check(spec)
        high_issues = [i for i in issues if i.get("severity") == "high"]
        if high_issues:
            print(f"Quality issues detected: {len(high_issues)} high-severity")
            # In production: regenerate affected sections

        return self.format_markdown(spec)

# Usage
evidence = {
    "finding_1": "Market size for agentic AI reached $2.3B in 2024, growing 180% YoY.",
    "finding_2": "Enterprise adoption led by financial services (28%) and healthcare (22%).",
    "finding_3": "Key challenges: reliability (67%), security (54%), integration (45%).",
}

agent = ReportGenerationAgent(evidence)
report = asyncio.run(agent.generate(
    "Agentic AI Market Landscape 2024",
    report_type="market intelligence report",
    audience="investment analysts"
))
print(report[:2000])
Example 2 — Template-Based Report with Section Constraints

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

REPORT_TEMPLATES = {
    "security_audit": {
        "sections": ["Executive Summary", "Scope", "Methodology", "Findings", "Risk Assessment", "Recommendations"],
        "word_targets": [200, 150, 200, 800, 400, 600],
        "audience": "CISO and security team",
    },
    "competitive_analysis": {
        "sections": ["Market Overview", "Competitor Profiles", "Feature Comparison", "Market Positioning", "Strategic Recommendations"],
        "word_targets": [300, 600, 400, 300, 400],
        "audience": "product and strategy teams",
    }
}

def generate_section_with_constraints(
    section_title: str,
    evidence: str,
    target_words: int,
    audience: str,
    preceding_context: str = "",
) -> str:
    """Generate a report section with strict word count enforcement."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=target_words * 3,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"""
Write the "{section_title}" section for a report targeted at {audience}.
Target: exactly {target_words} words (±10%).

{"Context from preceding sections: " + preceding_context[:500] if preceding_context else ""}

Evidence to incorporate:
{evidence[:1500]}

Output the section text only, no heading needed.
"""}]
    )
    return response.content[0].text

def generate_report_from_template(
    template_name: str,
    evidence_by_section: dict[str, str],
    report_title: str,
) -> str:
    """Generate a complete report from a named template."""
    template = REPORT_TEMPLATES[template_name]
    sections = template["sections"]
    word_targets = template["word_targets"]
    audience = template["audience"]

    output_sections = []
    preceding = ""

    for section, words in zip(sections, word_targets):
        content = generate_section_with_constraints(
            section_title=section,
            evidence=evidence_by_section.get(section, "General knowledge."),
            target_words=words,
            audience=audience,
            preceding_context=preceding,
        )
        output_sections.append(f"## {section}

{content}")
        preceding = content[:500]  # provide context to next section

    return f"# {report_title}

" + "

".join(output_sections)

# Usage
report = generate_report_from_template(
    template_name="competitive_analysis",
    evidence_by_section={
        "Market Overview": "AI code assistant market: $1.2B (2024), $4.8B projected (2026)...",
        "Competitor Profiles": "GitHub Copilot: 1.3M paid users. Cursor: 300K users. Tabnine: enterprise focus...",
    },
    report_title="AI Code Assistant Competitive Landscape Q4 2024",
)
print(report[:2000])

A global consulting firm generates client-facing strategy reports using a report generation agent integrated with their internal knowledge base and research systems. Consultants perform the analytical work — client interviews, competitive research, financial modelling — and log findings into a structured evidence repository tagged by section theme (market dynamics, competitive landscape, strategic options). The report agent runs nightly, picking up any new evidence added that day and updating the draft report with new sections or revised paragraphs.

The agent produces first drafts that consultants estimate are 70% complete and require 2-3 hours of editing rather than 2-3 days of writing from scratch. The most edited sections are always Strategic Recommendations — the section requiring the most interpretive judgment that the agent generates more conservatively than human consultants. Evidence for quantitative sections (financial analysis, market sizing) is extracted directly from spreadsheet exports, reducing transcription errors significantly.
Generating without an outline first: without a plan, sections repeat information and the document lacks coherent flow.
Single-context generation: trying to write a long report in one LLM call results in truncation, repetition, and loss of structural integrity.
No cross-section consistency check: sections written independently may contradict each other without a review pass.
Hardcoding formatting in prompts: embedding Markdown syntax in prompts produces fragile output; separate content generation from formatting.
Ignoring citation provenance: every factual claim must link to an evidence key; uncited claims enable hallucination.
Plan the outline first with an explicit structure; include target word counts per section to guide length.
Write sections in parallel to reduce latency; pass preceding-section summaries as context for continuity.
Run a consistency-checking pass on the assembled draft before delivery.
Use structured evidence stores with explicit keys; inject citations by key rather than by free-text search.
Implement section-level quality gates (length, citation density, readability score) before assembling the final document.
Version control report drafts and track which evidence items were used in each version for audit purposes.
Long context for assembly + review; structured output
Fills report templates with generated section content
Programmatic page creation in Notion workspaces

Report generation agents that produce regulatory filings or compliance documents require strict content validation before delivery. Implement a factual consistency checker that verifies every cited statistic appears verbatim in the referenced evidence key. Use LLM-as-judge with a separate model to evaluate factual accuracy of each section against the evidence store.
Never allow the report generator to access systems outside the evidence store it was given — prevent scope creep.
Watermark AI-generated drafts clearly until human review; never deliver AI reports as final without human sign-off in regulated contexts.
Log the full evidence store hash and model version alongside each report for reproducibility and audit.
Implement content classification to prevent sensitive evidence (PII, trade secrets) from appearing in reports destined for external audiences.
Report generation agents follow a plan-write-review cycle: outline first, then parallel section writing, then consistency checking.
Parallel section generation reduces report writing time from minutes to seconds at equivalent quality.
Evidence stores with explicit keys enable accurate citation injection and prevent hallucination in factual sections.
A consistency-checking pass on the assembled draft is essential — independent section generation creates contradictions without it.
Report quality is bounded by input evidence quality; thin evidence produces thin reports regardless of prompt sophistication.
Human review is required before delivering AI-generated reports in regulated or high-stakes contexts.
